weaknesses.title: Session Fixation
weaknesses.description: The product authenticates a user or establishes a new session without invalidating any existing session identifier, giving an attacker the opportunity to steal authenticated sessions. In a session fixation attack, the attacker obtains a valid session identifier (by creating a new session or intercepting one), forces the victim to authenticate using that session identifier, and then hijacks the now-authenticated session. This differs from session hijacking in that the attacker sets the session ID before authentication rather than stealing it after. Session fixation is possible when the application does not regenerate session identifiers upon authentication state changes.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test whether the session identifier changes after successful authentication by comparing the session cookie before and after login. Check if the application accepts session identifiers set by the client (via URL parameters, hidden form fields, or cookies). Verify that session identifiers are regenerated on privilege escalation, role changes, and other security state transitions. Test whether pre-authentication session data persists into the authenticated session. Review session management code for explicit session regeneration calls at authentication boundaries.
weaknesses.remediation: Regenerate the session identifier immediately after any change in authentication state: login, logout, privilege escalation, role change, or impersonation. Invalidate the old session completely on the server side. Do not accept session identifiers from URL parameters or form fields; only use secure cookies. Set session cookies with HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax attributes. Implement session binding to client characteristics (IP address range, user agent) as defense-in-depth. Use framework-provided session management that handles regeneration automatically.

weaknesses.title: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
weaknesses.description: The product sets sensitive cookies without the Secure attribute, allowing them to be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections. When session cookies or other authentication cookies lack the Secure flag, they will be sent in plaintext if the user navigates to any HTTP URL on the same domain, even if the authentication was performed over HTTPS. An attacker on the same network can intercept these cookies through passive eavesdropping or by injecting HTTP redirects. This is particularly dangerous in mixed-content environments and on shared networks.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Inspect Set-Cookie headers on authentication responses to verify the Secure attribute is present on all session cookies and tokens. Use browser developer tools to check cookie attributes. Test whether cookies are sent over HTTP by making an HTTP request to the same domain and monitoring network traffic. Verify that all authentication-related cookies (session ID, CSRF token, remember-me token) include the Secure flag. Check for mixed content that could cause cookie leakage. Use automated security scanners that check cookie security attributes.
weaknesses.remediation: Set the Secure attribute on all cookies that contain session identifiers, authentication tokens, CSRF tokens, or any sensitive data. Configure this at the framework level rather than per-cookie to prevent omissions. Implement HSTS to ensure all requests use HTTPS, preventing HTTP-based cookie interception. Additionally set HttpOnly to prevent JavaScript access and SameSite=Lax or SameSite=Strict to prevent cross-site cookie leakage. Consider using the __Host- cookie prefix for additional security guarantees. Regularly audit cookie configurations in security testing.

weaknesses.title: Use of Persistent Cookies Containing Sensitive Information
weaknesses.description: The product stores sensitive information in persistent cookies that survive browser sessions, increasing the window of opportunity for cookie theft. Persistent cookies (those with an Expires or Max-Age attribute) are stored on disk and survive browser restarts, making them vulnerable to theft through malware, physical access to the device, browser extension access, and forensic recovery. When session identifiers or authentication tokens are stored in persistent cookies, a single instance of device compromise can provide long-term access to the user's account.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Inspect Set-Cookie headers for authentication-related cookies with Expires or Max-Age attributes. Check if session cookies persist across browser restarts. Verify that 'remember me' functionality uses separate persistent tokens rather than extending the session cookie lifetime. Test whether persistent tokens can be revoked server-side. Review cookie storage on disk for sensitive data in plaintext. Check for persistent cookies that contain user roles, permissions, or other authorization data.
weaknesses.remediation: Use session cookies (without Expires/Max-Age) for authentication session identifiers so they are deleted when the browser closes. If 'remember me' functionality is required, implement it with a separate persistent token that: references a server-side record (not containing the session state itself), can be individually revoked, is rotated on each use, has a maximum lifetime of 30 days, and requires re-authentication for sensitive operations. Never store user roles, permissions, or personal information in persistent cookies. Implement device management allowing users to view and revoke persistent sessions.

weaknesses.title: Missing Encryption of Sensitive Data
weaknesses.description: The product does not encrypt sensitive data at rest or in transit, exposing it to unauthorized access through storage compromise, network interception, or memory dumping. In IAM contexts, sensitive data includes passwords, authentication tokens, session identifiers, personal identity information, security questions and answers, MFA secrets, and API keys. Failure to encrypt this data means that any breach of the storage medium or network path directly exposes the raw sensitive data without any additional protective barrier.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review data storage for sensitive data stored without encryption: check database columns for plaintext credentials, examine configuration files for cleartext secrets, inspect log files for inadvertent sensitive data logging, and audit backup storage for unencrypted copies. Test network communication for cleartext sensitive data transmission. Check memory handling for sensitive data that remains in memory after use. Verify that encryption is applied at the appropriate layer (application-level encryption for specific fields, storage encryption for volumes).
weaknesses.remediation: Implement encryption for all sensitive data at rest and in transit. Use TLS 1.2+ for all network communication carrying sensitive data. Encrypt sensitive database fields using application-level encryption (AES-256-GCM) with proper key management via HSM or cloud KMS. Use full-disk encryption or cloud-native storage encryption as a baseline. Implement proper key lifecycle management: generation, rotation, storage in HSM/KMS, and destruction. Classify data sensitivity to determine encryption requirements. Never log sensitive data; implement structured logging that explicitly excludes sensitive fields.

weaknesses.title: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
weaknesses.description: The product transmits sensitive information in cleartext over a communication channel that can be sniffed by unauthorized actors. This weakness specifically addresses data-in-transit protection and is distinct from CWE-523 (Unprotected Transport of Credentials) by covering all sensitive data, not just credentials. In IAM contexts, this includes session tokens, personal identity information, security question answers, MFA codes, and authorization decisions transmitted between services without encryption.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Capture and analyze network traffic between all system components to identify cleartext sensitive data transmission. Check for unencrypted protocols (HTTP, FTP, Telnet, SMTP, LDAP) used to transmit sensitive data. Verify that internal service-to-service communication uses TLS or mTLS. Test for TLS downgrade vulnerabilities. Check for sensitive data in URL query parameters (visible in logs and referrer headers). Review API responses for excessive data exposure that includes sensitive fields not needed by the client.
weaknesses.remediation: Enforce TLS 1.2+ on all communication channels carrying sensitive data, including internal service-to-service communication. Use mTLS for inter-service authentication and encryption. Replace all cleartext protocols: LDAPS instead of LDAP, SFTP/SCP instead of FTP, SSH instead of Telnet, SMTPS instead of SMTP. Never include sensitive data in URL query parameters; use request bodies or headers instead. Implement data minimization: only transmit the sensitive fields actually needed by the recipient. Use certificate transparency and monitoring to detect unauthorized certificates.

weaknesses.title: Plaintext Storage of a Password
weaknesses.description: The product stores a password in plaintext as part of an account's data in a database, file, or other storage medium. Plaintext password storage means that any breach of the storage medium directly exposes all user credentials with zero additional effort from the attacker. This is the most severe form of credential storage weakness. Despite being a well-known vulnerability, plaintext password storage continues to be discovered in production systems, sometimes due to logging, debugging, caching, or migration processes that inadvertently preserve plaintext copies.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review database schemas and application code for password storage. Check if passwords are stored directly in user tables without hashing. Examine log files, temporary files, and caches for plaintext password leakage. Review data migration scripts that may preserve plaintext copies during hash upgrades. Check LDAP userPassword attributes for plaintext storage ({CLEAR} or no prefix). Scan application memory dumps for cleartext password buffers. Verify backup systems do not contain plaintext credential exports.
weaknesses.remediation: Never store passwords in plaintext under any circumstances. Hash all passwords using Argon2id (recommended), bcrypt (cost 12+), or scrypt before storage. Implement transparent hash migration for legacy systems: when users authenticate against plaintext passwords, immediately hash and store the password, then delete the plaintext copy. Audit all data stores, logs, caches, and temporary files for plaintext password exposure. Implement data loss prevention (DLP) rules that detect and alert on potential plaintext password storage. After fixing, force password resets for all affected accounts.

weaknesses.title: Storing Passwords in a Recoverable Format
weaknesses.description: The product stores password credentials using reversible encryption rather than one-way hashing, allowing passwords to be recovered from the stored form. If passwords can be decrypted, then a compromise of the encryption key exposes all stored passwords simultaneously. This is fundamentally different from proper password hashing where even with full database access, each password must be individually cracked. Reversible password storage is sometimes implemented to support legacy systems that require the plaintext password, password synchronization between systems, or password recovery features.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review password storage code to determine if reversible encryption (AES, 3DES, RSA) is used instead of one-way hashing. Check for password decryption functions in the codebase. Verify that the 'forgot password' feature sends a reset link rather than the original password. Check LDAP schemas for password storage with reversible encryption configured. Look for base64 or other encoding of passwords that may appear to be encryption but is easily reversible. Review Active Directory policies for 'Store passwords using reversible encryption' settings.
weaknesses.remediation: Replace reversible password encryption with one-way password hashing using Argon2id, bcrypt, or scrypt. If legacy systems require the actual password, redesign the integration to use token-based authentication, service-to-service authentication, or credential vaulting rather than reversible password storage. Disable the 'Store passwords using reversible encryption' setting in Active Directory. If password recovery is required, implement password reset flows that generate new passwords rather than recovering old ones. Implement credential synchronization using modern protocols (SCIM) rather than shared password storage.

weaknesses.title: Use of Hard-coded Password
weaknesses.description: The product contains a hard-coded password used for authentication or for protecting resources. Hard-coded passwords are static credentials embedded directly in source code, configuration files checked into version control, or compiled into binaries. Unlike CWE-798 which covers all credential types, CWE-259 specifically addresses passwords. These passwords cannot be changed without modifying the source code and redeploying, they are identical across all installations, and they are discoverable through reverse engineering, source code access, or public repository exposure.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Run secret scanning tools (Gitleaks, TruffleHog, detect-secrets) against the current codebase and full git history. Search source code for password literals in authentication logic, database connection strings, and service configurations. Review compiled binaries using strings analysis for embedded passwords. Check Docker images and container layers for hardcoded credentials. Scan IaC templates (Terraform, ARM, CloudFormation) for hardcoded passwords. Review CI/CD pipeline configurations for exposed credentials.
weaknesses.remediation: Remove all hard-coded passwords from source code, configuration files, and container images. Use runtime secret injection from secure vault systems (Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). For development environments, use environment variables or local secret files that are gitignored. Implement pre-commit hooks (Gitleaks, detect-secrets) to prevent credential commits. Rotate all previously hard-coded passwords as they must be assumed compromised. Use managed identities or workload identity federation to eliminate passwords entirely for service-to-service authentication.

weaknesses.title: Password in Configuration File
weaknesses.description: The product stores passwords in configuration files that may be accessible to unauthorized users through file system access, backup access, or configuration management exposure. Configuration files often have broader read permissions than code files and are frequently included in backups, version control, configuration management systems, and deployment artifacts. Even when the configuration file itself is protected, its contents may be exposed through error messages, debug output, or configuration management APIs.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Scan configuration files for password patterns, connection strings, API keys, and other secrets. Check version control history for accidentally committed configuration files with secrets. Review file permissions on configuration files containing passwords. Check whether configuration management tools (Ansible, Puppet, Chef) store passwords in plaintext in their repositories. Verify that configuration files are excluded from public-facing error messages and debug output. Check container images for secrets in environment files or configuration layers.
weaknesses.remediation: Move passwords from configuration files to dedicated secret management systems (Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Use environment variables for configuration that references secrets stored in vaults. Implement configuration file templates that reference secret keys rather than containing secret values. Encrypt configuration files that must contain sensitive data using tools like SOPS, age, or cloud KMS. Set restrictive file permissions (0600) on any configuration files that contain sensitive data. Add configuration files with secrets to .gitignore and implement pre-commit scanning.

weaknesses.title: Weak Encoding for Password
weaknesses.description: The product uses a weak encoding scheme such as Base64, URL encoding, or XOR to obscure passwords before storage, providing no real security. Encoding is not encryption; it is a reversible transformation that provides no confidentiality. Developers sometimes use encoding to make passwords 'not plaintext' in configuration files or databases, creating a false sense of security while the passwords remain trivially recoverable. Any encoding scheme can be reversed without a key, making it equivalent to plaintext storage from a security perspective.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review password storage for Base64-encoded values (identified by the = padding character and alphanumeric character set). Check for URL-encoded, hex-encoded, or ROT13 password storage. Look for XOR-based 'encryption' where the key is stored alongside the encrypted data. Verify that stored password values are proper cryptographic hashes by checking output length, format, and algorithm identifiers. Test whether stored passwords can be decoded without a key. Check LDAP for password attributes stored with weak encoding schemes.
weaknesses.remediation: Replace all encoding-based password storage with proper one-way cryptographic hashing using Argon2id, bcrypt, or scrypt. Encoding (Base64, hex, URL encoding) is not a security mechanism and must never be used as a substitute for hashing or encryption. If reversible protection is needed (e.g., for configuration passwords that must be used in cleartext), use authenticated encryption (AES-256-GCM) with proper key management through HSM or KMS, not encoding. Force password resets for all accounts stored with weak encoding and treat previously encoded passwords as compromised.

weaknesses.title: Use of Weak Hash
weaknesses.description: The product uses a weak cryptographic hash algorithm (MD5, SHA-1, or other algorithms with known collision or preimage vulnerabilities) for security-critical purposes such as password hashing, integrity verification, or digital signature validation. Weak hash algorithms can be broken using collision attacks, preimage attacks, or brute force with modern GPU hardware. MD5 collisions can be generated in seconds, and SHA-1 collisions have been demonstrated in practice (SHAttered attack). Using these algorithms for password hashing, even with salting, provides inadequate protection against offline cracking attacks.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review cryptographic hash usage throughout the codebase for MD5, SHA-1, and other weak algorithms in security-critical contexts. Check for MD5 hash format (32 hex characters) or SHA-1 format (40 hex characters) in database password columns. Review digital signature validation for SHA-1 algorithm acceptance. Check SAML implementations for SHA-1 signature algorithm support. Analyze dependencies for known weak hash usage. Use SAST rules to flag calls to MD5/SHA-1 functions in security-critical code paths.
weaknesses.remediation: Replace MD5 and SHA-1 with SHA-256 or SHA-3 for general-purpose hashing needs. For password hashing, use purpose-built functions: Argon2id, bcrypt, or scrypt (not generic hash functions, even strong ones). For digital signatures, use SHA-256 or SHA-384 as the digest algorithm. Reject SAML assertions signed with SHA-1 and require SHA-256 minimum. Implement transparent hash migration: when users authenticate against legacy hashes, re-hash with the current algorithm. Set deprecation timelines for SHA-1 acceptance in all protocol implementations.

weaknesses.title: Inadequate Encryption Strength
weaknesses.description: The product uses encryption algorithms or key lengths that do not provide adequate protection for the sensitivity of the data being protected. In IAM contexts, this applies to encryption of stored credentials, session tokens, and authentication secrets using deprecated algorithms (DES, 3DES, RC4) or insufficient key lengths (RSA-1024, AES-128 for high-sensitivity data). Advances in computational power and cryptanalytic techniques continuously erode the security margins of encryption parameters, requiring periodic reassessment and migration to stronger configurations.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Audit all encryption configurations for algorithm and key length: check TLS cipher suites for weak algorithms, review application-level encryption for deprecated algorithms (DES, 3DES, RC4, Blowfish), verify RSA key lengths are at least 2048 bits (4096 recommended), and check AES configurations for 256-bit keys on high-sensitivity data. Scan TLS configurations with testssl.sh or SSL Labs. Review cryptographic library usage for hardcoded weak parameters. Check certificate key sizes and signing algorithms across all endpoints.
weaknesses.remediation: Standardize on AES-256-GCM for symmetric encryption and RSA-2048+ or ECDSA P-256+ for asymmetric operations. Disable all deprecated ciphers (DES, 3DES, RC4, export ciphers) in TLS configurations. For credential encryption at rest, use AES-256-GCM with keys managed through HSM or cloud KMS (Azure Key Vault, AWS KMS). Implement cryptographic agility: design systems to support algorithm migration without data loss. Establish a cryptographic standards document and enforce compliance through automated configuration scanning. Plan migration timelines aligned with NIST recommendations for algorithm deprecation.

weaknesses.title: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File
weaknesses.description: The product writes sensitive information such as passwords, session tokens, API keys, personal data, or authentication credentials into log files. Log files are typically stored with broad read access, retained for extended periods, transmitted to centralized logging systems, and may be accessible to operations staff, monitoring tools, and external log aggregation services. Sensitive data in logs creates a large, persistent attack surface where credential compromise can occur through log file access, log aggregation system breach, or insider threat.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Search log files and log aggregation systems for patterns matching credentials, tokens, passwords, session IDs, and personal data. Review logging configuration and code for explicit logging of authentication requests, responses, or error details that include sensitive data. Check for verbose debug logging that captures full HTTP requests or responses including authorization headers and cookie values. Test error handling paths to verify they do not log exception details containing credentials. Review structured logging schemas for sensitive field inclusion.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement structured logging with explicit field definitions that exclude sensitive data. Create a deny-list of fields that must never be logged: password, token, session_id, authorization header, cookie, secret, api_key, credit_card. Use logging frameworks that support field-level redaction or masking. Implement pre-logging sanitization that removes or masks sensitive data before it reaches the logging pipeline. Set log storage permissions to restrict access to authorized personnel only. Implement log rotation and retention policies that limit the exposure window. Review all error handling and exception logging for inadvertent sensitive data capture.

weaknesses.title: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
weaknesses.description: The product exposes sensitive information to an actor that is not explicitly authorized to have access to that information. In IAM contexts, this parent weakness covers many forms of sensitive data exposure including credential leakage in error messages, user enumeration through differential responses, session token exposure in URLs or referrer headers, internal system details in authentication error responses, and personal identity data accessible without proper authorization. This is one of the most frequently occurring weakness types in security assessments.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test all error responses for sensitive data leakage: submit invalid credentials and check if the response reveals whether the username or password was incorrect. Test API responses for excessive data exposure by comparing returned fields against what the user should see. Check error pages for stack traces, internal paths, or configuration details. Verify that authentication-related error messages are generic and identical for different failure modes. Test HTTP headers for sensitive information leakage (Server version, internal IPs, debug headers). Review referrer headers for token or session leakage.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement information minimization across all response channels. Return generic, identical error messages for authentication failures (never distinguish between invalid username and invalid password). Remove debug information, stack traces, and internal details from production error responses. Implement API response filtering that returns only fields the requesting user is authorized to see. Strip sensitive headers (Server, X-Powered-By) and internal details from responses. Use POST parameters or headers rather than URLs for sensitive data to prevent referrer leakage. Implement custom error pages that do not reveal system internals.

weaknesses.title: Use of GET Request Method With Sensitive Query Strings
weaknesses.description: The product uses HTTP GET request methods to transmit sensitive information via query string parameters, causing the sensitive data to be logged in web server logs, proxy logs, browser history, referrer headers, and potentially exposed in URL bars and bookmarks. In IAM contexts, this includes session tokens in URLs, OAuth2 access tokens as query parameters, password reset tokens in URLs without proper handling, and API keys passed as query parameters. GET request parameters are not protected by TLS in the URL itself relative to logging and browser history.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review all HTTP endpoints to identify those that accept sensitive data via GET query parameters. Check web server access logs for sensitive data in logged URLs. Verify that OAuth2 tokens are not passed as query parameters when header-based Bearer token authentication is available. Review password reset URLs to verify tokens are short-lived and single-use. Check proxy and CDN logs for sensitive query strings. Test whether session identifiers appear in URLs. Review referrer headers sent to third-party resources for leaked tokens or session IDs.
weaknesses.remediation: Transmit sensitive data using POST request bodies, HTTP headers (Authorization: Bearer), or cookies rather than GET query parameters. For OAuth2, use the Authorization header for access token transmission. For password reset tokens, ensure they are single-use, short-lived (15-30 minutes), and immediately invalidated after use. Configure web servers and proxies to strip or mask sensitive query parameters from access logs. Set Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin to prevent token leakage via referrer headers. Implement cache-control headers (no-store, no-cache) on responses containing sensitive URL parameters.

weaknesses.title: Use of Web Browser Cache Containing Sensitive Information
weaknesses.description: The product does not set proper cache-control directives on pages or API responses containing sensitive information, allowing the browser to cache authentication pages, personal data, or session-related information. Cached sensitive data persists on disk and can be accessed by other users of shared computers, recovered through forensic analysis, or exposed through browser extensions. In IAM contexts, this includes cached login pages with auto-filled credentials, cached authenticated pages with personal data, and cached API responses containing tokens.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Check HTTP response headers on pages containing sensitive data for proper cache-control directives. Verify that authentication pages, user profile pages, and API responses with sensitive data include Cache-Control: no-store, Pragma: no-cache, and Expires: 0. Test whether sensitive pages are retrievable from the browser cache after logout. Check for caching proxy configurations that may cache authenticated responses. Verify that CDN configurations exclude sensitive endpoints from caching.
weaknesses.remediation: Set Cache-Control: no-store on all responses containing sensitive data including authentication pages, user profiles, and API responses with tokens or personal data. Additionally set Pragma: no-cache for HTTP/1.0 compatibility and Expires: 0 to prevent proxy caching. Configure CDN and reverse proxy caching rules to never cache authenticated or sensitive responses. For APIs, ensure that responses vary based on the Authorization header to prevent shared caching of user-specific data. Implement clear-site-data header on logout to instruct browsers to clear cached data.

weaknesses.title: Use of Cache Containing Sensitive Information
weaknesses.description: The product uses a cache mechanism (in-memory cache, Redis, Memcached, CDN cache) that stores sensitive information without adequate access controls or expiration, potentially exposing the data to unauthorized users. In IAM systems, caching is commonly used for session data, authorization decisions, user profiles, and token validation results. When cache entries containing sensitive data are not properly scoped, expired, or access-controlled, they can be accessed by unauthorized code paths, leaked through cache poisoning, or persist beyond their intended lifetime.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review application caching architecture to identify what sensitive data is cached and where. Check Redis/Memcached configurations for authentication requirements and network exposure. Verify cache entry TTLs align with security requirements (session timeouts, token lifetimes). Test whether cached authorization decisions are properly invalidated when permissions change. Check for cache key enumeration vulnerabilities. Review CDN caching rules for sensitive API endpoint exposure. Verify that cache entries are tenant-scoped in multi-tenant applications.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement authentication and access controls on cache infrastructure (require AUTH for Redis, use SASL for Memcached). Encrypt sensitive data before caching using application-level encryption. Set appropriate TTLs on all cached sensitive data aligned with security requirements. Implement cache invalidation when security state changes (permission changes, session termination, token revocation). Scope cache keys to prevent cross-tenant access. Use dedicated cache instances or key prefixes for sensitive data. Implement cache poisoning protections and monitor for unauthorized cache access patterns.

weaknesses.title: External Control of Assumed-Immutable Web Parameter
weaknesses.description: The product relies on web parameters that are assumed to be immutable (hidden form fields, cookies, or URL parameters) for making security-critical decisions such as authentication state, role assignment, pricing, or resource authorization. Attackers can modify any client-side parameter using browser developer tools, intercepting proxies, or automated scripts. In IAM systems, this manifests as role or permission information stored in hidden fields or cookies that can be modified to escalate privileges, bypass authentication steps, or impersonate other users.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Identify all hidden form fields, cookie values, and URL parameters used in security decisions. Test by modifying these values to determine if the server validates them or trusts client-supplied data. Check for role, permission, or user ID values in hidden fields or cookies that can be tampered with. Test whether modifying a user ID parameter in a request changes the authorization context. Review JavaScript code for security logic that can be bypassed client-side. Check for HMAC or signature protection on security-critical parameters.
weaknesses.remediation: Never trust client-supplied data for security decisions. Maintain all security state server-side in secure session stores. If security-relevant parameters must traverse the client, protect them with cryptographic integrity verification (HMAC-SHA256 with a server-side secret). Validate all client-supplied parameters against server-side state on every request. Use opaque session identifiers as the only client-side authentication token, with all role, permission, and authorization data stored server-side. Implement server-side validation that rejects requests where client-supplied security parameters do not match server-side state.

weaknesses.title: Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains
weaknesses.description: The product uses a cross-domain policy (CORS, crossdomain.xml, clientaccesspolicy.xml) that allows access from untrusted or insufficiently restricted domains, enabling cross-origin attacks against authentication and session management. Overly permissive CORS configurations on authentication endpoints allow malicious websites to read authentication responses, steal tokens, and perform authenticated actions on behalf of the user. The most dangerous misconfiguration is reflecting the Origin header in Access-Control-Allow-Origin with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test CORS configuration on authentication and session-related endpoints by sending requests with various Origin headers and checking if they are reflected. Check for Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. Test with Origin: null which some CORS implementations accept. Review crossdomain.xml and clientaccesspolicy.xml for overly permissive domain policies. Verify that CORS preflight requests are properly validated. Check whether CORS headers are consistent across load-balanced instances.
weaknesses.remediation: Configure CORS with an explicit allowlist of trusted origins, never use wildcard (*) with credentialed requests. Validate the Origin header against the allowlist on every request rather than reflecting the client-supplied value. Set Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true only for specific trusted origins that require cookie-based cross-origin access. Remove unnecessary cross-domain policy files. Implement strict CORS on authentication endpoints with minimal allowed methods and headers. Use SameSite cookie attributes as additional protection against cross-origin cookie sending. Regularly audit CORS configurations across all services.

weaknesses.title: Sensitive Cookie with Improper SameSite Attribute
weaknesses.description: The product sets a sensitive cookie without a proper SameSite attribute, or sets it to SameSite=None without the Secure flag, making the cookie vulnerable to cross-site request attacks. The SameSite attribute controls when cookies are sent with cross-origin requests. Without SameSite protection, session cookies and authentication tokens are sent with cross-site requests initiated by malicious websites, enabling CSRF attacks and cross-site information leakage. Browsers have moved toward defaulting to SameSite=Lax, but explicit configuration remains critical for security-sensitive applications.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Inspect Set-Cookie headers on all authentication and session-related responses to verify SameSite attribute presence and value. Check for SameSite=None without the Secure attribute (browsers will reject this combination). Verify that sensitive cookies use SameSite=Lax at minimum, with SameSite=Strict for highly sensitive operations. Test cross-site request scenarios to verify cookie behavior. Review framework and middleware cookie configuration for default SameSite settings. Check for cookies that require cross-origin sending (legitimate SSO flows) and verify they use SameSite=None with Secure.
weaknesses.remediation: Set SameSite=Lax on all session cookies and authentication tokens as the default. Use SameSite=Strict for cookies associated with highly sensitive operations (banking, admin functions) where cross-site navigation is not required. Only use SameSite=None when cross-site cookie sending is required (federated SSO, embedded iframes) and always pair with the Secure attribute. Configure SameSite at the framework level to ensure consistent application across all cookies. Implement CSRF tokens as defense-in-depth alongside SameSite attributes. Document which cookies require SameSite=None and the justification for cross-site sending.

weaknesses.title: Sensitive Cookie Without 'HttpOnly' Flag
weaknesses.description: The product sets a cookie containing sensitive information such as a session identifier or authentication token without the HttpOnly flag, making it accessible to client-side JavaScript. When cookies lack the HttpOnly attribute, they can be stolen through cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks using document.cookie, enabling session hijacking. XSS is one of the most prevalent web vulnerabilities, and HttpOnly cookies are a critical defense-in-depth measure that limits the impact of XSS by preventing direct cookie theft even when script injection succeeds.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Inspect Set-Cookie headers on all responses that set session cookies, authentication tokens, and CSRF tokens to verify the HttpOnly flag is present. Use browser developer tools to check cookie attributes. Test whether JavaScript (document.cookie) can access authentication cookies from the browser console. Review framework cookie configuration for default HttpOnly settings. Check if any legitimate JavaScript functionality requires direct cookie access that might have led to HttpOnly being omitted. Verify HttpOnly is set consistently across all application instances and load balancers.
weaknesses.remediation: Set the HttpOnly flag on all cookies containing session identifiers, authentication tokens, and CSRF tokens. Configure HttpOnly at the framework or middleware level to ensure it is applied consistently. If client-side JavaScript requires access to certain values, use separate non-sensitive cookies or JavaScript-accessible tokens (e.g., XSRF-TOKEN pattern) while keeping the primary session cookie HttpOnly. Combine HttpOnly with Secure and SameSite attributes for comprehensive cookie security. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) as additional defense against XSS-based cookie theft.

weaknesses.title: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information in a Cookie
weaknesses.description: The product stores sensitive information in a cookie in cleartext, allowing it to be read by any entity with access to the cookie including the user, browser extensions, cross-site scripts, and network observers (if the Secure flag is missing). In IAM contexts, this includes storing user roles, permission levels, personal information, or internal identifiers in cookies without encryption or integrity protection. Even when the information is not a direct credential, it can aid in privilege escalation, account enumeration, or targeted attacks.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Decode all cookie values (Base64, URL encoding) and inspect for sensitive information such as user roles, email addresses, internal user IDs, permission flags, or personal data. Check for JWT tokens stored in cookies that contain sensitive claims in the payload (JWTs are Base64-encoded, not encrypted, unless using JWE). Verify that cookie values cannot be tampered with to change authorization state. Test whether modifying cookie values affects the application's behavior or displayed data. Review cookie content before and after authentication for newly added sensitive values.
weaknesses.remediation: Never store sensitive information directly in cookies. Use opaque session identifiers that reference server-side session stores containing the actual data. If data must be stored client-side, use encrypted and authenticated tokens (JWE with AES-256-GCM) or signed tokens (JWS with RS256) that cannot be read or modified by the client. Minimize the data stored in client-side tokens to only what is strictly necessary. Implement server-side validation of all security-relevant claims rather than trusting cookie-stored values. Consider using the __Host- cookie prefix for additional origin-binding guarantees.
